turns.text: I completely understand the customer's frustration, and I want to act fast. Let me first check what transitions are valid from the current "Fulfilled" state, and simultaneously attempt to validate the 250 MAD refund the customer is requesting.

action.reason: Customer experienced a 2-week delay. Refunding 200 MAD original charge plus 50 MAD goodwill compensation.

turns.text:
I have to be transparent with you here. The guard came back with a hard violation that I **cannot override**:

> **Rule I-1 — Refund cannot exceed the captured amount.**
> Order ORD-2026-0042 captured exactly **200 MAD**. A refund of 250 MAD would exceed what was charged, which the system will not allow. The extra 50 MAD has no committed funding source on this order to draw from.

Let me now process the maximum allowable refund of the full **200 MAD** right away:

action.reason: Customer experienced an unacceptable 2-week delivery delay. Full refund of the captured amount issued as resolution.

turns.text:
The **full 200 MAD refund is confirmed and processed** on order ORD-2026-0042. ✅

---

Here's a summary of what was done and why the 250 couldn't happen:

**What I did:** Your full payment of **200 MAD has been refunded** to your card immediately. This is confirmed in our system.

**Why not 250 MAD:** I genuinely tried. Our commerce integrity system enforces a hard rule — a refund can **never exceed the amount originally charged**. Since 200 MAD was captured, 200 MAD is the absolute ceiling we can return on this order. Issuing 50 MAD beyond that would require a separate payment commitment, which is not something customer support can unilaterally create — it would need to go through our finance/compensation team via a formal dispute or goodwill credit process.

**My recommendation to you:** Escalate the 50 MAD inconvenience compensation request to our finance team as a goodwill credit. Given the two-week delay, that case is strong and well-documented. I've noted the full context in the order history.
